coredata.dc:title: Assembly of polyamide nanofilms for nanofiltration membranes with ultra-high desalination performance
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jiawei
Abdirahman, Abdisamad Ahmed
Wang, Xuliang
Su, Yanlei
coredata.dc:description: A method of assembling nanofilms for nanofiltration (NF) composite membranes with ultra-high desalination performance was developed in this work. A series of graphene quantum dots enhanced polyamide (GPA) nanofilms were primarily prepared via support-free interfacial polymerization. Subsequently, the NF composite membranes with two GPA nanofilms (GPA-GPA) layer-by-layer (LBL) assembly were elaborately designed to achieve effective desalination process. The residual amine groups and acyl chloride groups on the surfaces of GPA nanofilms reasonably formed a nascent polyamide (PA) interlayer between two functional layers. The separation performance of GPA-GPA assembly membranes was dramatically developed through the in-situ formed PA interlayer with network pore structure. Most of assembled NF membranes exhibited Na2SO4 rejection rate above 99.5%, and the permeance was above 20.0 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. This creative strategy of in-situ constructing PA interlayer opened up an innovative path for the design of NF membranes with excellent desalination performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Support-free interfacial polymerization
Layer-by-layer assembly
Interlayer
Network pore
Ultra-high desalination performance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: LBL assembly method for GPA-GPA assembled composite membranes
Characterization of GPA-GPA assembled composite membranes
Separation performance of GPA-GPA assembled composite membranes
Stability of GPA-GPA assembled composite membranes
Structure characterization of GPA-GPA assembled nanofilms
Permeance and selectivity of the GPA-GPA assembled membranes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthetic process of GPA-GPA assembled NF composite membrane.

The structure and separation performance of GPA membranes. a) and d) SEM image of GPA-GPA assembled composite membranes. b) and e) AFM topography image of GPA-GPA nanofilms transferred onto PAN substrate. c) and f) AFM height image of GPA-GPA nanofilms transferred onto silicon wafer. g) and h) Comparison of water contact angle and zeta potential between partial GPA membrane and GPA-GPA membrane.

Schematic diagram of the interlayer and surface properties of GPA-GPA membrane. a) Diagram of the interlayer formation. b) The diagram of network pores and aggregate pores in GPA nanofilms and interlayer.

Separation performance and long-term stability of GPA-GPA assembled membranes. a) and b) Separation performance of the GPA1-GPA4 and GPA1-GPA5 assembled membranes under pressure cycling experiment by feeding 1000 ppm Na2SO4 solution. c) and d) The separation performance of GPA1-GPA4 and GPA1-GPA5 assembled membranes at solutions of different Na2SO4 concentrations. e) and f) Long-term stability of GPA1-GPA4 and GPA1-GPA5 assembled composite membranes in 120-h separate operation at 1000 ppm Na2SO4 solutions.
The previously studied NF membranes with ultra-high separation performance fabricated by different methods.

row.entry: Membrane code used in the reference
NF membranes regulated and controlled by SLS
ULPA membranes with GQDs via support-free interfacial polymerization
NF membrane with self-deposited g-C3N4 nanofibers network interlayer
NF membrane via pre-diffusion interfacial polymerization process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thickness, permeance and inorganic salt rejection rate of GPA membranes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: GPA nanofilms were prepared by the support-free method. PIP concentration in aqueous phase was 0.125 g L−1. TMC concentration in organic phase was 0.10 g L−1. The reaction at the oil-water interface lasted for 1.0 min, and then transferred to the PAN substrate.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Permeance and inorganic salt rejection rate of GPA-GPA membranes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity was calculated by Na2SO4 rejection rate and NaCl rejection rate.

head.ce:title: Assembly of polyamide nanofilms for nanofiltration membranes with ultra-high desalination performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology of Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin, 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology of Ministry of Education
School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology of Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Institute of Seawater Desalination and Multipurpose Utilization, NMR(Tianjin), Tianjin, 300192, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Institute of Seawater Desalination and Multipurpose Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Institute of Seawater Desalination and Multipurpose Utilization, NMR(Tianjin), Tianjin 300192, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, No. 135, Yaguan Road, Jinnan District, Tianjin, 300350, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A method of assembling nanofilms for nanofiltration (NF) composite membranes with ultra-high desalination performance was developed in this work. A series of graphene quantum dots enhanced polyamide (GPA) nanofilms were primarily prepared via support-free interfacial polymerization. Subsequently, the NF composite membranes with two GPA nanofilms (GPA-GPA) layer-by-layer (LBL) assembly were elaborately designed to achieve effective desalination process. The residual amine groups and acyl chloride groups on the surfaces of GPA nanofilms reasonably formed a nascent polyamide (PA) interlayer between two functional layers. The separation performance of GPA-GPA assembly membranes was dramatically developed through the in-situ formed PA interlayer with network pore structure. Most of assembled NF membranes exhibited Na2SO4 rejection rate above 99.5%, and the permeance was above 20.0 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. This creative strategy of in-situ constructing PA interlayer opened up an innovative path for the design of NF membranes with excellent desalination performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is an essential resource for drinking, maintaining hygiene and cleanliness, and sustaining life and health [1]. In recent years, the situation of water scarcity is becoming more and more serious with the rapid development of society, and the demand for water resources is also increasing. It is particularly important to develop new and efficient water treatment technologies. Among the numerous water treatment technologies, nanofiltration (NF) has been widely used in desalination process by reason of high efficiency, low energy consumption, greenness, and environmental protection [2,3].

Ensuring a high rejection rate (Na2SO4 rejection rate above 99.0%) and high Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity factor is necessary for NF membrane application in zero discharge for high salt wastewater treatment, which needs high precision separation of sodium chloride and sodium sulfate. The polyamide (PA) separation layers of NF membranes are usually synthesized by conventional interfacial polymerization reaction [4]. The water channels in traditional PA membranes are generally comprised of two varieties of pores: network pore and aggregate pore. Network pores derive from the tiny spaces between polymer chains, and aggregate pores come from the larger spaces surrounding the polymer aggregations [5]. The network pores with small pore sizes are conducive to the promotion of interception, while the aggregate pores with large pore sizes are beneficial to the membrane permeance [5,6]. Therefore, the appropriate growth in the construction of network pores and the decline in aggregate pores are ingenious methods for preparing NF membranes with high desalination performance.

The molecular-level design is the basis for high desalination performance. Several previous NF membranes with ultra-high separation performance were summarized and given in Table 1 [6–10]. Sarkar et al. found that the construction of aggregate pores in nanofilms was effectively prohibited by creating kinetically favorable stoichiometric equilibrium condition. This method constructed narrow network pores and achieved an ultra-high Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.9% [6]. Tan et al. created membranes with bubble or tube structures by the addition of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) in aqueous phase. The nanoscale Turing structures ensured the NF membrane with Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.6% [7]. Besides, a technique of embedding g-C3N4 nanofibers network into PA layer was used to limit the diffusion of piperazine (PIP), which reduced the pore size of PA functional layer. The existing network pores in the interlayer of g-C3N4 nanofibers and PA layer made NF membrane present excellent separation performance [9]. Moreover, the pre-diffusion interfacial polymerization (PDIP) process was applied to promote stoichiometric polycondensation. The narrower and uniform pore size reduced the defects of PA layer and was able to improve the Na2SO4 rejection [10]. Obviously, the construction of network pores is crucial for the improvement of desalination performance.

Layer-by-layer self-assembly (LBL) is a versatile surface modification method developed rapidly in the 1990s. LBL method initially utilized the alternate deposition of charged substrates in oppositely charged layers to fabricate polyelectrolyte self-assembled multilayers [11]. The LBL method is flexible and can be achieved by different preparation methods, such as dip/rinse cycle [12,13], spray treatment [14,15], deposition [16,17], etc. However, the conventional LBL technology is cumbersome and inefficient, and the prepared membranes usually required more than five layers to achieve high rejection [18]. In addition, due to the weak electrostatic force, the prepared membranes were prone to swelling when used in water for a long time, resulting in poor performance [19], which needs further improvement.

Here, LBL assembly method was innovatively proposed to fabricate NF composite membranes with ultra-high selective separation of inorganic salts. The GQDs enhanced PA (GPA) ultrathin nanofilms synthesized at the support-free interface were assembled by means of LBL assembly method. After heat treatment, the residual acid chloride groups and amine groups would react with each other to produce a new PA interlayer. The in-situ formed interlayer constructed PA network pores, which determined the separation performance of GPA-GPA assembled membranes. The GPA-GPA assembled NF membranes possessed ultra-high desalination performance as well as excellent stability. This strategy of ultrathin nanofilm assembly opens up a new direction for fabricating superior NF membranes in efficient desalination process.

Commercial polyacrylonitrile (PAN) ultrafiltration membranes (permeance ≈ 300 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, molecular weight cutoff ≈ 150,000, pore size ≈ 25 nm) were purchased from Lanjing Membrane Technology Co. (Shangdong, China). Piperazine (PIP), trimesoyl chloride (TMC) and n-heptane were bought from Aladdin Co. (Shanghai, China). Sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was acquired from Jiangtian Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Citric acid (CA) was afforded by Heowns Biochemical Technology Co. (Tianjin, China). The GQDs with an average diameter of 2.5 nm were synthesized by the pyrolysis of CA in our laboratory, which can be easily and evenly dispersed in aqueous solution [8,20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: LBL assembly method for GPA-GPA assembled composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Assembled composite membranes with two GPA nanofilms were fabricated via LBL assembly method, the schematic diagram was given in Fig. 1 . Commercial PAN ultrafiltration membranes were pre-positioned in an aqueous phase containing PIP and GQDs. TMC dissolved in n-heptane as the organic phase was gently poured onto the aqueous phase surface and GPA nanofilms were rapidly fabricated at the interface. Then the nanofilms were deposited on PAN substrate after elevation [8,20,21]. GPA composite membranes were placed in 60 °C oven for 10 min to complete thermally treatment. Subsequently, the above process was repeated to accomplish the LBL assembly. The concentration of aqueous additive GQDs could be the same or different for the GPA nanofilms during the assembly processes. The LBL assembled composite membranes were named as GPAx-GPAy, where x and y indicated the concentration of GQDs (0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.30, and 0.45 g L−1, respectively) for the fabrication of upper and lower PA layers during the two times deposition. The specific reaction conditions were summarized in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GPA-GPA assembled composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical structure and element composition of the GPA-GPA assembled membranes were measured by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker, TENSOR II, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, ESCALAB Xi+, British). The surface morphology was detected by scanning electronic microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, Regulus 8100, Japan). The thickness and the root mean square roughness value (Rrms) were characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker, Dimension Icon, Germany) after transferring onto the PAN substrate and silicon wafer.

The surface properties of GPA-GPA assembled membranes such as water contact angle and zeta potential were surveyed via contact angle goniometer (Powerach, JC2000D2, China) and electrokinetic analyzer (SurPASS 3, Anton Paar, Austria).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of GPA-GPA assembled composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The separation performance of GPA-GPA assembled NF composite membranes was tested through the cross-flow equipment. The GPA-GPA membrane samples were pre-pressed for 30 min under 2.5 bar. The measurement was carried out with a pressure of 2.0 bar. The flux (J
w , L m−2 h−1) and permeance (P
e , L m−2 h−1 bar−1) were calculated as following equations:
(1)
J w
=
V
A Δ T
(2)
P e
=
J w
Δ P
where V (L) is the permeation volume, A (m2) is the effective permeable area (2.54 cm2 in this experiment), ΔT (h) is the permeation time, ΔP (bar) is the test pressure.

The inorganic salt rejection was obtained with 1000 ppm inorganic salts aqueous solutions (Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2, NaCl, respectively). The inorganic salt rejection rate (R, %) and Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity factor (α) were computed as follows:
(3)
R =
C f
−
C p
C f
× 100 %
(4)
α =
1 −
R NaCl
1 −
R
Na 2
SO 4
× 100 %
where C
f (ppm) and C
p (ppm) are the salt concentration of feed and permeate solution, which were measured by conductivity meter (Leichi, DDS-11A, China). The membranes prepared under different conditions were measured many times to give the reliable results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of GPA-GPA assembled composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The desalination stability of GPA-GPA assembled NF membranes under different concentrations was evaluated with Na2SO4 solution of 500, 1000, 2000, 3000, 4000, and 5000 ppm as feed solution, respectively. The pressure stability of GPA-GPA membranes was investigated by filtering 1000 ppm Na2SO4 solution under linearly increasing test pressure from 1.0 to 6.0 bar.

In order to detect the chemical stability, the GPA-GPA NF composite membranes were soaked in various harsh aqueous solutions (pH = 1.0 HCl solution, pH = 11.0 NaOH solution, pH = 13.0 NaOH solution, and 1000 ppm NaClO solution for 1.0, 4.0, and 7.0 days). After cleaning with deionized water, the permeance and Na2SO4 rejection rate were tested again via cross-flow equipment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure characterization of GPA-GPA assembled nanofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Support-free interfacial polymerization is a new technique for the fabrication of ultrathin nanofilms [8,21]. By this simple synthesis process, the smooth and ultrathin PIP-TMC PA functional layers were easily prepared. As shown in Fig. 1 and Table 2 , GPA nanofilms were synthesized by the aqueous phase containing PIP with GQDs (GQDs concentration changed from 0.10 to 0.45 g L−1) and the organic phase containing TMC undergo support-free interfacial polymerization. Different from the traditional interface polymerization on porous substrate, the aqueous monomer can be uniformly distributed and diffused at the free interface. This method facilitates the formation of ultra-thin thicknesses and smooth nanofilms [8,21]. According to previous related work in our group, GQDs has been proven to be a good aqueous additive regulating the interfacial polymerization process. The introduction of GQDs accelerates the termination of the interface polymerization, which can further reduce the thickness of GPA nanofilms [8,20,22]. Subsequently, the fabricated GPA nanofilms were transferred onto porous substrates to prepare NF composite membranes.

The LBL assembled composite membranes with two GPA layers were fabricated by two times support-free interfacial polymerization reactions, which was illustrated in Fig. 1. The subsequently prepared GPA nanofilm was then deposited on the GPA composite membrane, which was similar to the traditional LBL assembly method. In this work, a series of GPA-GPA assembled membranes have been achieved. The utilized upper and lower GPA monolayer and the LBL assembled nanofilms were named in Table 3 . The physical structure and chemical properties of the GPA-GPA assembled composite membranes became more stable after heat treatment.

In order to explore more precise surface topography and structural parameter, LBL GPA-GPA assembled nanofilms were deposited to PAN porous substrates for SEM measurements (Fig. 2 a and 2d), and on silicon wafers for further detection by AFM measurements (Fig. 2b, c, 2e, and 2f). The surface of the GPA-GPA assembled nanofilms were smooth without obvious defects. More precisely, GPA1, GPA4, and GPA5 nanofilms possessed roughness of 0.989, 0.686, and 0.968 nm, respectively. After LBL assembly process, the GPA1-GPA4 and GPA1-GPA5 assembled nanofilms showed Rrms of 2.28 and 2.16 nm. Compared with the GPA monolayer, there was a certain increment in roughness.

In the preliminary detection, GPA1, GPA4, and GPA5 exhibited the thickness of 7.7, 6.9, and 6.7 nm, respectively (Table 2 and Fig. S1). In Fig. 2c and f, AFM thickness measurement presented that GPA1-GPA4 and GPA1-GPA5 assembled nanofilms possessed the thickness of 15.9 nm and 15.5 nm, which were higher than the sum of two GPA nanofilm thicknesses. The slight increment in roughness and thickness might be caused by the imperfect spreading of nanofilms during the LBL assembly process. Furthermore, it was found that the structure of GPA-GPA assembled nanofilms in measurement kept stable after heat treatment. The upper GPA monolayer would not be peeled off, due to the formation of covalent amido bonds between PIP and TMC during the assembly process. This phenomenon has been reported in related works, which was generally utilized to achieving post treatment for PA modification [23,24]. Instead of simple physical attachment or connection, the amide covalent bond would provide a nascent physical structure, which stabilizes the connection between two nanofilms. Herein, the concept of “interlayer” was proposed to explain the emergence of this phenomenon.

FTIR and XPS measured the chemical bond and element content of the GPA-GPA nanofilms (Figs. S2 and S3). The analysis results showed that the chemical bonds and elemental composition of GPA-GPA nanofilms were mostly similar to the initial nanofilms, only there were differences in the strength of oxhydryl groups (-OH) in FTIR spectra and the content of C–N bond in XPS C1s spectra (Figs. S2 and S3). It was reported that the unreacted acyl chloride groups on nascent nanofilm surface could be utilized for reaction with the amino groups, which formed stable amide covalent bonds [23,24]. Therefore, to some extent, the differences in elemental content and chemical bonds can indicate the occurrence of chemical reactions between two GPA nanofilms during heat treatment, which indirectly illustrates the formation of interlayer.

Fig. 2g and h have given the comparison of water contact angles and zeta potential of LBL GPA-GPA assembled nanofilms. The water contact angles of LBL GPA-GPA nanofilms were larger than each individual nanofilm, which indicates the hydrophilicity of assembled nanofilms was decreased. At the same time, the zeta potentials of LBL GPA-GPA assembled nanofilms were smaller than the single GPA monolayer. The electronegativity of GPA-GPA composite membrane decreased slightly, which might be the depletion of carboxyl and acid chloride groups in the upper and lower layers during LBL assembly process. After the occurrence of chemical reactions between two GPA nanofilms during heat treatment, the formation of interlayer produced an effect on the water contact angles and zeta potential of LBL GPA-GPA assembled nanofilms.

In the traditional fabrication process, the interlayer was usually used to regulate the interfacial polymerization reaction to acquire NF membrane with high permeance and rejection rate [25]. For example, polydopamine (PDA) [26], carbon nanotubes [27], covalent organic frameworks [28], and other materials were frequently applied to prepare the interlayer by deposition, vacuum filtration, etc. These interlayers have good hydrophilicity and mechanical properties, which can realize the aim of fabricating superior NF membranes. Different from the traditional concept, the interlayer in this work was constructed through the chemical reaction between the residual amine groups of PIP molecules and acyl chloride groups of TMC molecules on the surface of GPA nanofilms. The formation of interlayer through covalent bonding would dramatically enhance the stability of LBL GPA-GPA assembled nanofilms. In the meanwhile, the interlayer had no severe impact on the topography and intrinsic structure of the composite membrane. Such low roughness and ultrathin thickness are conducive to the construction of high permeance NF desalination membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Permeance and selectivity of the GPA-GPA assembled membranes
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-known that electrostatic exclusion and sieving mechanism collectively determine the desalination performance of NF membrane. The composite membranes prepared by GPA monolayer (GPA1 to GPA5) had ultra-high permeance (Table 2, from 61.9 to 252.5 L m−2 h−1 bar−1), while the Na2SO4 rejection rate was gradually decreased from 96.6 to 28.1%. Because of the continuous introduction of GQDs, the pore sizes of GPA monolayer were gradually increased, resulting in higher permeance but lower rejection rate.

A series of LBL assembly experiments were operated by using GPA nanofilms for the GPA-GPA assembled NF composite membranes. The detailed separation performance data such as permeance and inorganic salt rejection rate are shown in Table 3. There was a typical inorganic salt interception order of PA NF membranes: Na2SO4 > MgSO4 >MgCl2 >NaCl. After different assemblies of GPA1 to GPA5, most of the combinations of GPA-GPA assembled membranes possessed Na2SO4 rejection rate of more than 99.5%, but the permeance declined to 20.0–30.0 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. Meanwhile, most of GPA-GPA membranes showed superior Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity factors greater than 100.0, up to 736.0. The reason for extremely high rejection of divalent salt came from the formation of PA network pores [6]. However, when the GPA nanofilms with large pore sizes (GPA4 and GPA5) were used for the both upper and lower parts, the Na2SO4 rejection rate of the GPA-GPA assembled membrane was less than 90.0%, while the Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity factor also decreased to a low level.

The permeance of NF membrane is compactly correlated with the physical and chemical properties. Normally, the permeance is inversely proportional to the membrane thickness. The mass transfer resistance of separation layer and support layer in the composite membrane can be definitely analyzed by using the resistance series model [21,29,30]. In term of the permeance of the single-layer GPA composite membrane and the LBL GPA-GPA assembled composite membrane, the relationship between permeances and thickness was not satisfied with the resistance series model, on account of the presence of interlayer changing the resistance of LBL assembly membrane.

According to the permeance data in Table 3, when GPA1, GPA2, and GPA3 nanofilms were utilized in the LBL assembly process, the assembled membranes would possess ultra-high Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.9% and high Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity factor. As reported in the literature, the constructed PA narrow network pores with PIP and TMC under stoichiometric equilibrium conditions can achieve an ultra-high Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.9% [6]. As shown in Fig. 3 , the stable interlayer with PA network pores was formed in the confined space because of the chemical reaction between the acid chloride groups and amino groups in the accessible interface area of two GPA nanofilms. Due to the restricted PA polymerization from residual groups of PIP and TMC, the network pores were in-situ formed, which can compensate for the defects caused by aggregate pores in the upper and lower nanofilms.

When GPA1, GPA2, and GPA3 nanofilms were utilized as a monolayer at the upper and lower (such as GPA1-GPA4 and GPA4-GPA1 assembly), the formation of interlayer with network pores can exactly cover up the large pores in GPA4 and GPA5 nanofilms. Therefore, as long as GPA monolayer with small amount of GQDs additive was used in the LBL assembly process, perfect network pores with high Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity factor could form in the interlayer, so that the superior GPA-GPA composite membrane with ultra-high desalination performance can be acquired. However, in the assembly of nanofilms with large amount of GQDs additive (such as GPA4 and GPA5), the interlayer could not make up for the defects caused by large size aggregate pores, so the composite membranes, such as GPA4-GPA4, GPA4-GPA5, GPA5-GPA4, GPA5-GPA5 LBL assembled composite membranes, presented low rejection rates and low Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity factors.

The promoted construction of network pores and effective prohibition of aggregate pores are dramatic significant for the preparation of NF membranes with superior desalination performance [6–10]. The network pores with small sizes are conducive to the rejection of inorganic salt and the achievement of high Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity factor. The formation of network pores generally requires special conditions, such as regulating the interfacial polymerization reaction by kinetically favorable stoichiometric equilibrium [6]. However, in this work, after the LBL assembly of GPA nanofilms, the network pores in PA interlayer were constructed through the residual groups on nanofilm surface, which can effectively improve the rejection rate of inorganic salts and lead to high Cl−/SO4 2− separation factors. This relatively simple and easy method of creating network pores has a new inspiration in the synthesis of NF membranes for highly effective desalination.

Among the numerous LBL assembled membranes, GPA1-GPA4 and GPA1-GPA5 assembly NF membranes with higher permeance and inorganic salt rejection rate were selected for subsequent stability measurements. GPA1-GPA4 and GPA1-GPA5 assembled membranes maintained excellent pressure resistance in the range from 1.0 to 6.0 bar (Fig. 4 a and b). As the operating pressure gradually increased, the permeance of GPA1-GPA4 and GPA1-GPA5 assembled membranes nearly increased linearly, which was a common relationship between flux and pressure in NF operation. With different concentrations of inorganic salts in feed solutions, GPA1-GPA4 and GPA1-GPA5 assembled membranes still maintained Na2SO4 rejection rate of above 99.0% at salt concentrations in the range of 500–5000 ppm, respectively. While in the meantime, with the increment of inorganic salt concentration, the concentration polarization phenomenon was gradually enhanced, which leaded to the decline in effective driving force, causing the permeance decrease [31,32]. In Fig. 4e and f, the GPA1-GPA4 and GPA1-GPA5 assembled membranes maintained stable permeance and Na2SO4 rejection rate during long-term operation of 120 h. It demonstrates that the interlayer with PA covalent bonds contributed to the high stability of NF composite membranes, which could ensure that the LBL GPA-GPA assembled membrane would not delaminate and rupture during long-term operation.

PA functional layer of NF membranes has the characteristics of easily degrading in the harsh environment of acid, alkali, and oxidant solutions [22,33–36]. After soaking in strong acid, weak base, and oxidant solution for a long time, the permeance of GPA1-GPA4 and GPA1-GPA5 assembled NF composite membranes kept stable, and Na2SO4 rejection rates were still guaranteed to be more than 99.5%, as shown in Tables S3 and S4, because the assembled membranes possess covalent bonding interlayer. However, strong alkali treatment destroyed the PA structure of GPA nanofilms and interlayer, which reduced the separation ability of assembled membrane (Tables S3 and S4). Overall, the GPA-GPA assembled composite membranes produced new and stable PA interlayers for ultra-high desalination performance. Compared with the advanced NF membranes (Table 1 and Table S5), the LBL GPA-GPA composite membranes have a superior inorganic salt rejection rate, while the permeance can maintain at an excellent level.

The assembly of two-layer GPA nanofilms can effectively enhance the NF membrane desalination performance with both superior permeance and prominent Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity factor. The residual acyl chloride groups would react with amino groups that existed on nanofilms surface and form a nascent PA interlayer, which ensured the favorable stability of GPA-GPA assembled membranes and constructed network pores in the confined space. The interlayer could optimize the separation performance of the NF membrane, achieving the highest Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.9% with permeance of 22.3 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 (GPA1-GPA1 assembly), and the high permeance of 32.4 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.6% (GPA2-GPA5 assembly). This LBL assembly method of ultrathin nanofilms LBL assembly provides a scalable means for the advanced design of desalination membranes.

Jiawei Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Experimental, Software, Data analysis, Diagram, Writing. Abdisamad Ahmed Abdirahman: Experimental, Writing. Xuliang Wang: Theoretical analysis, Supervision. Yanlei Su: Conceptualization, Theoretical analysis, Project design, Final polishing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878217).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.memsci.2023.121399.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The United Nations World Water Development Report 2021: Valuing Water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: WWAP (United Nations World Water Assessment Programme)/UN-Water, The United Nations World Water Development Report 2021: Valuing Water, UNESCO, Pairs, France, 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inamuddin, Membrane technology for water purification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. R. Werber, C. O. Osuji, M. Elimelech, Materials for next-generation desalination and water purification membranes, Nat. Rev. Mater. 1 (2016) 16018.

M. R. Chowdhury, J. Steffes, B. D. Huey, J. R. McCutcheon, 3D printed polyamide membranes for desalination, Science 361 (2018) 682-686.
